name: busybox-on-windows
description: How to use a Win32 build of BusyBox to run many of the standard UNIX command line tools on Windows.
capabilities: Provides step-by-step instructions for downloading BusyBox on Windows
Explains CPU architecture detection for correct binary selection
Documents common BusyBox commands and usage patterns
References official BusyBox documentation and resources
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill is a documentation file (markdown) that provides instructions for installing and using BusyBox on Windows. Static analyzer flagged 27 patterns, but all are false positives. Markdown code backticks were misidentified as Ruby shell execution. Documented PowerShell commands are legitimate administrative examples for users to run manually. URLs point to official BusyBox distribution sites (frippery.org, busybox.net). Registry queries read OS version information only. No executable code exists in this skill.
valueStatement: Windows users lack native Unix command-line tools. This skill provides a complete guide to install and use BusyBox, bringing familiar Unix commands to Windows environments.
userTitle: Run Unix Commands on Windows with BusyBox
limitations: Only applicable to Windows operating systems
Requires manual execution of PowerShell commands by user
Does not provide automated installation scripts
BusyBox feature set is limited compared to full Unix tools

useCases.title: Developer Environment Setup
useCases.description: Enable Unix command familiarity for developers transitioning between Windows and Linux environments

useCases.title: Scripting and Automation
useCases.description: Use familiar Unix tools in Windows batch scripts and automated workflows

useCases.title: Learning Unix Commands
useCases.description: Practice Unix command-line skills on Windows without dual-booting or virtualization

promptTemplates.title: Basic Setup Query
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me install BusyBox on my Windows 11 machine. I have a 64-bit Intel processor.

promptTemplates.title: Command Discovery
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to list files with BusyBox on Windows, similar to the Unix ls command.

promptTemplates.title: Architecture Detection
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I determine my Windows system architecture to download the correct BusyBox binary?

promptTemplates.title: Advanced Usage
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to run BusyBox commands from a different working directory. Explain how to use absolute paths with busybox.exe.

bestPractices: Verify your CPU architecture before downloading the BusyBox binary
Download BusyBox only from official sources (frippery.org or busybox.net)
Use absolute paths when running BusyBox from different directories
antiPatterns: Do not download BusyBox from unofficial third-party sources
Avoid running PowerShell commands without verifying CPU architecture first
Do not assume all Unix commands have identical behavior in BusyBox

faq.question: What is BusyBox and why use it on Windows?
faq.answer: BusyBox is a single executable that combines many common Unix utilities. It brings familiar command-line tools to Windows without requiring WSL or Cygwin.

faq.question: Which BusyBox version should I download for my system?
faq.answer: Use busybox64.exe for 64-bit x86 systems, busybox.exe for 32-bit x86, and busybox64a.exe for ARM64 systems. Check your architecture with the PowerShell CPU detection command.

faq.question: Do I need administrator privileges to use BusyBox?
faq.answer: No, BusyBox runs as a regular user application. However, some commands may require elevated privileges depending on the operations performed.

faq.question: Can I use BusyBox commands in PowerShell scripts?
faq.answer: Yes, you can call busybox.exe from PowerShell scripts just like any other executable. Prefix commands with busybox.exe or add it to your PATH.

faq.question: How does BusyBox compare to WSL on Windows?
faq.answer: BusyBox is a lightweight single-binary solution with limited commands. WSL provides a full Linux environment but requires more resources and setup.

faq.question: Where can I find documentation for BusyBox commands?
faq.answer: Official documentation is available at frippery.org/busybox/ and busybox.net. Use busybox.exe --list to see available commands.
